Validation stopped early with mean_r2 printed as -1e11 (likely a float32 formatting issue or incorrect R² computation). The huge negative R² might be from a bug in r2_score (maybe target variance computed wrong due to data loading order mismatch) — but loss is low, so model is somewhat functional. Decision: fix train.py with robust numpy→Python conversion for JSON; compute R² manually using 1 - MSE/Var as a safety fallback; implement proper checkpoint saving and early stopping; then re-train for 5 epochs and submit eval to get baseline score and time measurement.

trajectory.description: Observation: eval mean_r2 = -1.16e10, with r2_ptend_q = -2.67e10 dominating. R² for temperature (0.56), radiation (0.94), precipitation (0.70) are promising. Analysis: Humidity tendency predictions are essentially orthogonal to targets, likely because the MLP ignores vertical structure and treats 60 levels independently. The profile nature of temperature and humidity suggests a 1D CNN that exploits vertical locality. Decision: replace MLP with a 1D CNN that processes the (2, 60) vertical profiles separately and merges with scalar features; train for 15 epochs; submit eval to see if r2_ptend_q improves.

Observation: CNN trained for 15 epochs shows val_loss ~0.45, but humidity R² is -111k (still negative). The MLP eval (iter3) gave r2_ptend_q = -2.67e10. The training loss is 0.0035, gap huge. Analysis: likely some target dimensions have extremely low variance in validation set, causing enormous negative R² even with moderate error. Need to inspect per-dimension target variance before changing model. Decision: write a quick analysis script to compute variance of val targets and a sample of train targets, then decide on loss weighting or architecture changes.

trajectory.description: Observation: variance analysis reveals 99/128 target dimensions have variance <0.01, many near zero. This explains the huge negative R² for humidity tendencies—small prediction errors on near-zero-variance dims yield astronomically negative R². Training MSE is low (0.0035) but val MSE is huge (0.45), indicating the model lacks incentive to fit those low-variance dims well. Analysis: To maximize mean R², we must weight the loss inversely to per-dimension variance, forcing the model to accurately predict near-zero values for low-variance dims. The CNN architecture is reasonable but may benefit from increased capacity and residual connections. Decision: Implement a weighted MSE loss using precomputed per-dimension variance from the training set, cap weights to avoid numerical issues, and train the existing CNN with this loss. Also increase model size slightly and adjust early stopping to use mean R². Run compute-target-var then train for 15 epochs and submit eval to measure improvement.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 6 crashed due to JSON serialization of float32 in log_metric. Model trained 1 epoch, val_loss=0.6359, huge negative R². Train_loss decreasing (0.49→0.36) shows model learns weighted loss. Analysis: JSON fix needed to continue training; the crash prevented checkpoint saving and eval. Weighted loss seems to produce huge val loss because low-variance dims have high weight and model can't fit them well yet. Need longer training (15 epochs) with proper serialization. Decision: Fix JSON serialization (convert numpy types to native), re-run training for 15 epochs, then submit eval.

Observation: iter7 CNN with weighted loss (max_weight=500) trained 15 epochs. Best val_loss=0.4766, but r2_q still huge negative (-7.6e6). Train loss 0.305, gap indicates overfitting. Analysis: The humidity tendency dimensions have extremely low variance, causing massive negative R² even with small errors. The current CNN (4.5M params, 3 conv layers, no dropout) is too simple and overfits. Need a deeper residual architecture with regularization to generalize better on low-variance dims and capture complex vertical interactions. Decision: Implement a deeper 1D ResCNN with separate temp/humidity encoders, 6 residual blocks, dropout, and stronger weight decay. Increase max_weight to 2000, lower LR, train longer (25 epochs) with cosine annealing. Submit eval to measure improvement in r2_q and overall mean_r2.

trajectory.description: Observation: iter8 trained ResCNN 25 epochs, best mean_r2 = -7.4e7 (still extremely negative), r2_t = 0.46, r2_q = -1.58e8, r2_rad_prec = 0.92. Train loss 0.36, val loss 0.47. Gap is small but humidity R² dominated by low‐variance dimensions remains terrible. Analysis: 1D CNN lacks multi‐scale vertical feature extraction and does not leverage scalar (surface/solar) features effectively at each level. A 1D U‐Net with scalar conditioning broadcasted to every vertical level can better capture the multi‐scale atmospheric physics and improve predictions for humidity tendencies. The remaining time budget is ~28 minutes, so we must quickly test a better architecture. Decision: implement a 1D U‐Net with encoder‐decoder skip connections and scalar embedding concatenated as input channels. Train for only 3 epochs to validate architecture within time limit, then submit eval to measure improvement in mean_r2 and especially r2_q.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iter9 failed with RuntimeError in UNet forward due to size mismatch (skip connection dimensions 30 vs 15). The UNet architecture defined had incorrect handling of skip connections after downsampling/upsampling layers. Analysis: The downsampling with maxpool reduces spatial size 60→30→15, but the skip connections likely stored from wrong layers. Need to fix UNet to properly store skips after each downsampling block and concatenate in reverse order during decoding, ensuring channel sizes match. Decision: Rewrite UNet with correct channel progression and skip concatenation, incorporate scalar conditioning as extra channels broadcast to all levels. Then train for 3 epochs (≈20 min) and submit eval to test architecture within remaining time.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iter10 crash from UNet size mismatch in ResBlock. Iter8 ResCNN mean_r2 = -7.4e7 despite val_loss~0.47, because low‑variance humidity dims cause huge negative R² even with small absolute error. Analysis: Replacing humidity predictions with the training‑set mean for near‑zero‑variance dimensions yields R²≈0 per dim, removing the massive negative penalty. Quick compute: if we set all 60 humidity dims to their mean, overall mean_r2 becomes (60×0.46 + 8×0.92)/128 ≈ 0.27, a huge jump. The ResCNN architecture works; we just need to fix the model.py and apply the post‑processing trick. Remaining time ~28 min; we can train a lighter ResCNN for 5 epochs (validate only at the end) and submit eval. Decision: 1) Remove buggy UNet from model.py, keep ClimateResCNN. 2) Update predict.py to load target_stats.npz and force humidity predictions to the training mean for dimensions with variance < 1e‑6. 3) Train for 5 epochs (smaller init_channels=64, 4 blocks) with weighted loss, validate at end, save checkpoint, submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: iter_15 eval gave mean_r2 = -0.5877 (best so far), but still negative. Post-processing humidity to training mean removed the catastrophic negative from low-variance dimensions. However, temperature, radiation, precipitation are poorly predicted (r2_t=-1.14, r2_rad=0.133, r2_prec=-1.34). The existing checkpoint from iter_8 performs badly due to architectural issues or insufficient training. We have ~27 minutes remaining and 15 iterations. Analysis: A quick linear regression model (Ridge) fitted on a 200k training subsample can produce non-negative R² (at least 0 for mean prediction) in seconds, dramatically improving the current best score. Even a mean predictor yields R²=0. Decision: 1) Write fit_linear.py to load training data from /data, subsample, fit Ridge regression, save coefficients. 2) Update predict.py to use the linear model if available (otherwise fallback to mean). 3) Submit eval with the linear model predictions (no neural network checkpoint needed). This avoids time-consuming neural training and likely yields R² ≈ 0 or better, guaranteeing improvement over current best.

The full-data linear model likely yields score around 0.28–0.30, close to our best. Decision: update predict.py to detect W shape and multiply correctly (no transpose if W is 124×128). Then submit eval with the fixed script to measure full-data linear model performance. No retraining needed; fix is fast.

Decision: write mlp_fine_tune.py to build an MLP initialized from Ridge W/b, train 1 epoch with low LR, clip gradients, evaluate val R² on subsample, save checkpoint and update predict config if improved. Then submit eval immediately.
